annotations.label: Topologically distinguishable points

anchor.snippet: two points x and y in X are topologically distinguishable

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the complementary relation `Inseparable` (same neighborhood filters); topologically distinguishable corresponds to `¬ Inseparable x y` but is not given its own name.

annotations.label: Separated points

anchor.snippet: Two points x and y are separated if each of them has a neighbourhood that is not a neighbourhood of the other

annotations.note: No dedicated predicate for two points being separated (having mutually non-contained neighborhoods) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Separated subsets

anchor.snippet: two subsets A and B of X are separated if each is disjoint from the other's closure

annotations.note: Mathlib's `SeparatedNhds` (disjoint open neighborhoods) is strictly stronger; the closure-disjoint notion appears only as `SeparatedNhds.disjoint_closure_left/right` consequences.

annotations.label: Separated by neighbourhoods

anchor.snippet: Subsets A and B are separated by neighbourhoods if they have disjoint neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `SeparatedNhds s t` is defined as the existence of disjoint open sets containing `s` and `t` respectively.

annotations.label: Separated by closed neighbourhoods

anchor.snippet: They are separated by closed neighbourhoods if they have disjoint closed neighbourhoods

annotations.note: No standalone predicate for disjoint closed neighborhoods; only appears unfolded inside `T25Space` and `exists_nhds_disjoint_closure`.

annotations.label: Separated by a continuous function

anchor.snippet: They are separated by a continuous function if there exists a continuous function f from the space X to the real line

annotations.note: The notion is not packaged as a predicate but is realized by lemmas like `exists_continuous_zero_one_of_isClosed` producing the Urysohn-style separating function.

annotations.label: Precisely separated by a continuous function

anchor.snippet: they are precisely separated by a continuous function if there exists a continuous function f from X to

annotations.note: No predicate for `f⁻¹{0}=A` and `f⁻¹{1}=B` style precise separation appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: T0 (Kolmogorov) space

anchor.snippet: or Kolmogorov , if any two distinct points in X are topologically distinguishable

annotations.note: `T0Space` is defined as `Inseparable x y → x = y`, equivalent to distinct points being topologically distinguishable.

annotations.label: R0 (symmetric) space

anchor.snippet: or symmetric , if any two topologically distinguishable points in X are separated

annotations.note: `R0Space` is defined by symmetry of the specialization relation, equivalent to the Wikipedia condition.

annotations.label: T1 (accessible/Fréchet) space

anchor.snippet: or accessible or Fréchet , if any two distinct points in X are separated

annotations.note: `T1Space` is the standard typeclass with `IsClosed {x}` and equivalent reformulations.

annotations.label: T1 iff T0 and R0

anchor.snippet: Thus, X is T 1 if and only if it is both T 0 and R 0

annotations.note: The exact equivalence is stated at `Mathlib/Topology/Separation/Basic.lean:454`.

annotations.label: R1 (preregular) space

anchor.snippet: or preregular , if any two topologically distinguishable points in X are separated by neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `R1Space` requires that any two points either specialize or have disjoint neighborhoods, matching the preregular condition.

annotations.label: R1 implies R0

anchor.snippet: Every R 1 space is also R 0

annotations.note: An anonymous priority-100 `R0Space X` instance is given for `[R1Space X]` at `Mathlib/Topology/Separation/Basic.lean:889`.

annotations.label: Hausdorff (T2) space

anchor.snippet: is Hausdorff , or T 2 or separated , if any two distinct points in X are separated by neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `T2Space` is the standard Hausdorff typeclass with disjoint open neighborhoods of distinct points.

annotations.label: Hausdorff iff T0 and R1

anchor.snippet: Thus, X is Hausdorff if and only if it is both T 0 and R 1

annotations.note: Under `[R1Space X]`, `T2Space X ↔ T0Space X` is proved at line 135.

annotations.label: Hausdorff implies T1

anchor.snippet: Every Hausdorff space is also T 1

annotations.note: The instance `T2Space.t1Space` at line 115 derives `T1Space` from `T2Space`.

annotations.label: T2½ (Urysohn) space

anchor.snippet: or Urysohn , if any two distinct points in X are separated by closed neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `T25Space` requires disjoint closed neighborhoods of distinct points (formulated via `lift' closure` filters).

annotations.label: T2½ implies Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: Every T 2½ space is also Hausdorff

annotations.note: Priority-100 instance `T25Space.t2Space` at line 371 gives the implication.

annotations.label: Completely Hausdorff space

anchor.snippet: is completely Hausdorff , or completely T 2 , if any two distinct points in X are separated by a continuous function

annotations.note: No `CompletelyHausdorffSpace` / `CompletelyT2Space` typeclass exists in Mathlib; only the related `T35Space`-level lemma `separatesPoints_continuous_of_t35Space` appears.

annotations.label: Completely Hausdorff implies T2½

anchor.snippet: Every completely Hausdorff space is also T 2½

annotations.note: Without a `CompletelyHausdorff` class in Mathlib, this implication is not stated.

annotations.label: Regular space

anchor.snippet: is regular if, given any point x and closed set F in X such that x does not belong to F , they are separated by neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `RegularSpace` is defined via `Disjoint (𝓝ˢ s) (𝓝 a)` for closed `s` not containing `a`, matching the Wikipedia definition.

annotations.label: Regular implies R1

anchor.snippet: Every regular space is also R 1

annotations.note: Priority-100 `R1Space X` instance under `[RegularSpace X]` at `Mathlib/Topology/Separation/Regular.lean:165` (with comment `A regular space is R₁`).

annotations.label: Regular Hausdorff (T3) space

anchor.snippet: is regular Hausdorff , or T 3 , if it is both T 0 and regular

annotations.note: `T3Space` extends `T0Space` and `RegularSpace` (line 408).

annotations.label: Regular Hausdorff implies T2½

anchor.snippet: Every regular Hausdorff space is also T 2½

annotations.note: Priority-100 instance `T3Space.t25Space` at line 416.

annotations.label: Completely regular space

anchor.snippet: is completely regular if, given any point x and closed set F in X such that x does not belong to F , they are separated by a continuous function

annotations.note: `CompletelyRegularSpace` is defined via separation of a point from a closed set by a continuous `X → I` function.

annotations.label: Completely regular implies regular

anchor.snippet: Every completely regular space is also regular

annotations.note: Instance at line 86 derives `RegularSpace` from `CompletelyRegularSpace`.

annotations.label: Tychonoff (T3½) space

anchor.snippet: is Tychonoff , or T 3½ , completely T 3 , or completely regular Hausdorff , if it is both T 0 and completely regular

annotations.note: `T35Space` extends `T0Space` and `CompletelyRegularSpace` (line 231).

annotations.label: Tychonoff implies regular Hausdorff and completely Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: Every Tychonoff space is both regular Hausdorff and completely Hausdorff

annotations.note: `T35Space.instT3space` gives the T3 part and `separatesPoints_continuous_of_t35Space` (line 250) witnesses the completely Hausdorff conclusion, though the latter is not stated as an instance of a `CompletelyHausdorff` class.

annotations.label: Normal space

anchor.snippet: is normal if any two disjoint closed subsets of X are separated by neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `NormalSpace` is defined by `SeparatedNhds` of disjoint closed sets at line 465.

annotations.label: Urysohn's lemma

anchor.snippet: a space is normal if and only if any two disjoint closed sets can be separated by a continuous function

annotations.note: The forward direction of Urysohn's lemma (`NormalSpace → exists separating continuous function`) is proved as `exists_continuous_zero_one_of_isClosed`; the converse and packaged `↔` are not explicitly stated.

annotations.label: Normal regular space

anchor.snippet: is normal regular if it is both R 0 and normal

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `NormalRegularSpace` class; the compound assumption `[NormalSpace X] [R0Space X]` is used directly (e.g. in `NormalSpace.instCompletelyRegularSpace`).

annotations.label: Normal regular implies completely regular

anchor.snippet: Every normal regular space is also completely regular

annotations.note: Instance at line 95 derives `CompletelyRegularSpace` from `[NormalSpace X] [R0Space X]`.

annotations.label: Normal Hausdorff (T4) space

anchor.snippet: is normal Hausdorff , or T 4 , if it is both T 1 and normal

annotations.note: `T4Space` extends `T1Space` and `NormalSpace` (line 521).

annotations.label: Normal Hausdorff implies Tychonoff and normal regular

anchor.snippet: Every normal Hausdorff space is also both Tychonoff and normal regular

annotations.note: `T4Space.instT35Space` gives Tychonoff; T1 ⇒ R0 plus `NormalSpace` is the normal regular condition (no dedicated class).

annotations.label: Completely normal space

anchor.snippet: is completely normal if any two separated sets are separated by neighbourhoods

annotations.note: `CompletelyNormalSpace` is defined via the closure-disjointness hypothesis implying disjoint neighborhood filters (line 559).

annotations.label: Completely normal implies normal

anchor.snippet: Every completely normal space is also normal

annotations.note: Priority-100 instance at line 569.

annotations.label: Completely normal Hausdorff (T5) space

anchor.snippet: is completely normal Hausdorff , or T 5 or completely T 4 , if it is both completely normal and T 1

annotations.note: `T5Space` extends `T1Space` and `CompletelyNormalSpace` (line 640).

annotations.label: Completely normal Hausdorff implies normal Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: Every completely normal Hausdorff space is also normal Hausdorff

annotations.note: Priority-100 instance `T5Space.toT4Space` at line 654.

annotations.label: Perfectly normal space

anchor.snippet: is perfectly normal if any two disjoint closed sets are precisely separated by a continuous function

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `PerfectlyNormalSpace` as normal plus closed sets are Gδ — the standard equivalent characterization — but does not phrase it directly as 'precisely separated by a continuous function'.

annotations.label: Perfectly normal implies completely normal and completely regular

anchor.snippet: Every perfectly normal space is also both completely normal and completely regular

annotations.note: Instance at line 103 gives completely normal; combined with the priority-100 `[PerfectlyNormalSpace X] : R0Space X` at line 116 and `NormalSpace.instCompletelyRegularSpace`, completely regular follows.

annotations.label: Perfectly normal Hausdorff (T6) space

anchor.snippet: is perfectly normal Hausdorff , or T 6 or perfectly T 4 , if it is both perfectly normal and T 0

annotations.note: `T6Space` extends `T0Space` and `PerfectlyNormalSpace` (line 127).

annotations.label: Perfectly normal Hausdorff implies completely normal Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: Every perfectly normal Hausdorff space is also completely normal Hausdorff

annotations.note: Priority-100 instance `T6Space.toT5Space` at line 131.

annotations.label: Sober space

anchor.snippet: is sober if, for every nonempty closed set C that is not the union of two of its closed proper subsets

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `QuasiSober` (every irreducible closed set has a generic point); 'sober' (uniqueness of generic point) is the combination `[QuasiSober X] [T0Space X]` rather than a single class.

annotations.label: Hausdorff implies sober implies T0

anchor.snippet: Any Hausdorff space must be sober, and any sober space must be T 0

annotations.note: `R1Space.quasiSober` gives the (stronger) implication R1 ⇒ QuasiSober, hence Hausdorff ⇒ QuasiSober; the 'sober ⇒ T0' direction is built into the Mathlib convention that sober = QuasiSober + T0.

annotations.label: Weak Hausdorff space

anchor.snippet: is weak Hausdorff if, for every continuous map f to X from a compact Hausdorff space, the image of f is closed in X

annotations.note: No `WeakHausdorffSpace` class or equivalent definition appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hausdorff implies weak Hausdorff implies T1

anchor.snippet: Any Hausdorff space must be weak Hausdorff, and any weak Hausdorff space must be T 1

annotations.note: Without weak Hausdorff defined, these implications are not stated.

annotations.label: KC space

anchor.snippet: is a KC space if all compact subsets of X are closed

annotations.note: Mathlib has `IsCompact.isClosed` under `T2Space`, but no `KCSpace` typeclass abstracting the property.

annotations.label: KC strictly between T1 and T2

anchor.snippet: The KC space property is strictly between T 1 and T 2 (Hausdorff) in strength

annotations.note: Not stated; KC space is not formalized.

annotations.label: KC stronger than weak Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: The KC space property is strictly

annotations.note: Neither KC nor weak Hausdorff is formalized, so the implication is absent.

annotations.label: Semiregular space

anchor.snippet: is semiregular if the regular open sets form a base for the open sets of X

annotations.note: No `SemiregularSpace` class found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular implies semiregular

anchor.snippet: Any regular space must also be semiregular

annotations.note: Not formalized as semiregular is not defined.

annotations.label: Quasi-regular space

anchor.snippet: is quasi-regular if for any nonempty open set G , there is a nonempty open set H such that the closure of H is contained in G

annotations.note: No quasi-regular space typeclass found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fully normal space

anchor.snippet: is fully normal if every open cover has an open star refinement

annotations.note: No `FullyNormalSpace` class found in Mathlib; only `ParacompactSpace` is provided.

annotations.label: Fully T4 space

anchor.snippet: is fully T 4 , or fully normal Hausdorff , if it is both T 1 and fully normal

annotations.note: Not formalized as fully normal is not defined.

annotations.label: Fully normal implies normal; fully T4 implies T4

anchor.snippet: Every fully normal space is normal and every fully T 4 space is T 4

annotations.note: Not formalized since fully normal/fully T4 are absent.

annotations.label: Fully T4 implies paracompact

anchor.snippet: one can show that every fully T 4 space is paracompact

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes `ParacompactSpace` but not 'fully T4', so the equivalence/implication is not stated.

annotations.label: Cofinite topology: T1 KC counterexample

anchor.snippet: for the cofinite topology on infinitely many points, which is T 1 , every subset is compact but not every subset is closed

annotations.note: `CofiniteTopology` is defined and shown to be T1 (`t1Space_cofinite`), but the specific KC counterexample fact (compact ≠ closed) is not stated as KC is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cocountable topology: KC not Hausdorff

anchor.snippet: for the cocountable topology on uncountably many points, the compact sets are all finite and hence all closed but the space is not T 2

annotations.note: The cocountable topology and the KC-but-not-T2 example are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous image of compact Hausdorff in KC is closed

anchor.snippet: The image of a continuous map from a compact Hausdorff space is necessarily compact

annotations.note: `IsCompact.image` plus `IsCompact.isClosed` (under `T2Space`) realize this, but no statement uses a KC-codomain hypothesis since KC is not formalized.
